title: कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकम् ॥

वसुदेवसुतं देवं कंसचाणूरमर्दनम्।
देवकीपरमानन्दं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्।
श्रीकृष्णाय मङ्गलम्॥१॥

नन्दगोपकुमाराय गोविन्दाय नमो नमः।
गोपीजनवल्लभाय गोपालाय च मङ्गलम्॥२॥

वेणुवाद्यविशारदाय गोपालाय नमो नमः।
रुक्मिणीशाय राधेशाय कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥३॥

पीताम्बरधरं देवं कौस्तुभोद्भासिताङ्गदम्।
मुरलीधररूपाय कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥४॥

मथुरानाथमीशानं वृन्दावनविहारिणम्।
गोवर्धनगिरिधारिणं कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥५॥

कालियफणिमर्दनं बकासुरविमर्दनम्।
पूतनाप्राणहारिणं कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥६॥

रासलीलाविलासाय यमुनातीरवासिने।
बांसुरीधरमोहनाय कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥७॥

पार्थसारथये नित्यं गीताज्ञानप्रदायिने।
विश्वरूपप्रदर्शाय कृष्णाय च सुमङ्गलम्॥८॥

मङ्गलाष्टकमेतद्यो कृष्णस्य प्रपठेन्नरः।
सर्वपापविनिर्मुक्तो गोलोकं स गमिष्यति॥

॥ इति श्री कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Krishna Mangalashtakam ||

Vasudevasutam Devam Kamsa-chanura-mardanam.
Devaki-paramananadam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.
Shri Krishnaya Mangalam.||1||

Nandagopa-kumaraya Govindaya Namo Namah.
Gopi-jana-vallabhaya Gopalaya Cha Mangalam.||2||

Venu-vadya-visharadaya Gopalaya Namo Namah.
Rukminishaya Radheshaya Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||3||

Pitambara-dharam Devam Kaustubhodbhasitangadam.
Murali-dhara-rupaya Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||4||

Mathura-natham-Ishanam Vrindavana-viharinam.
Govardhana-giridharim Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||5||

Kaliya-phani-mardanam Bakasura-vimardanam.
Putana-prana-harinam Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||6||

Rasa-leela-vilasaya Yamuna-tira-vasine.
Bansuri-dhara-mohanaya Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||7||

Partha-sarathaye Nityam Gita-jnana-pradayine.
Vishvarupa-pradarshaya Krishnaya Cha Sumangalam.||8||

Mangalashtakam-Etadyo Krishnasya Prapathennnarah.
Sarva-papa-vinirmukto Golokam Sa Gamishyati.

|| Iti Shri Krishna Mangalashtakam Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
१. वसुदेवपुत्र, कंसचाणूरमर्दन, देवकी के परमानन्द, जगद्गुरु श्रीकृष्ण को मंगल।

२. नन्दगोपकुमार, गोविन्द, गोपीजनवल्लभ, गोपाल को मंगल नमस्कार।

३. वेणुवादनविशारद, गोपाल, रुक्मिणीश, राधेश कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

४. पीताम्बरधारी, कौस्तुभमणि से दीप्त, मुरलीधर कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

५. मथुरानाथ, वृन्दावनविहारी, गोवर्धनधारी कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

६. कालियनागमर्दन, बकासुरवध, पूतनाप्राणहारी कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

७. रासलीलाविलासी, यमुनातीरवासी, बांसुरीधर मोहन कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

८. पार्थसारथि, गीताज्ञानदाता, विश्वरूपदर्शक कृष्ण को सुमंगल।

जो इस कृष्ण मंगलाष्टक का पाठ करता है, वह सर्वपापमुक्त होकर गोलोक को जाता है।
benefits: Invokes the auspicious blessings of Lord Krishna
Covers all leelas from Vrindavan to Kurukshetra
Invokes liberation from all sins and attainment of goloka
Ideal for Janmashtami and Krishna-related festivals
Fills the heart with devotion and divine love
Powerful for new beginnings and auspicious occasions
description: The Krishna Mangalashtakam, known in Sanskrit as कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकम्, is an eight-verse devotional hymn in praise of Lord Krishna. This revered text originates from Bhagavata Purana tradition. Regular recitation is believed to grant liberation from all sins and attainment of Goloka, invoke the auspicious blessings of Lord Krishna, and fill the heart with devotion and divine love. It is especially recommended on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami and Holi for maximum spiritual benefit. At 8 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 5 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Krishna Mangalashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Krishna Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishna Mangalashtakam (कृष्ण मङ्गलाष्टकम्) is an eight-verse mangala-stotra in praise of Lord Krishna, drawn from the Bhagavata Purana tradition. The mangalashtakam genre comprises auspicious eight-verse hymns recited at the start of new ventures, weddings, festivals and worship to invoke divine blessings — each verse concludes with the refrain Krishnaya cha (su)mangalam — 'auspiciousness to Krishna.' This Krishna mangalashtakam uniquely traverses the entire span of Krishna's leelas, from his birth as Vasudeva-suta and slaying of Kamsa-Chanura, through his Vrindavan childhood as Govinda and Gopala, his Mathura kingship, his Dvaraka leela as Rukminisha, and finally his Pandava-friendship and Gita-upadesha as Partha-sarathi. (Source: Bhagavata Purana tradition; Krishna stotra corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the refrain 'Krishnaya cha mangalam' mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The refrain Krishnaya cha (su)mangalam concludes every verse of the Krishna Mangalashtakam. Krishnaya — 'to Krishna' (in the dative case); cha — 'and'; (su)mangalam — '(supreme) auspiciousness, blessing.' Together: 'May there be (supreme) auspiciousness to Lord Krishna.' This is the classical mangala-vachana of the Bhagavata tradition — invoking blessings upon the Lord himself, by which the devotee in turn becomes the recipient of those blessings. The refrain creates a meditative rhythm across the eight verses, with each Krishna leela invoked as a source of mangala for the chanter. (Source: Krishna Mangalashtakam refrain in verses 1-8.)

seoFaqs.question: Which Krishna leelas are praised in the Krishna Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The eight verses systematically traverse Krishna's life: V1 — Vasudeva-suta, slayer of Kamsa-Chanura, joy of Devaki; V2 — Nanda-gopa-kumara, Govinda, Gopi-jana-vallabha, Gopala; V3 — Venu-vadya master, Rukminisha, Radhesha; V4 — Pitambara-dhara, Kaustubha-radiant, Murali-dhara; V5 — Mathura-natha, Vrindavana-vihari, Govardhana-giridhari; V6 — Kaliya-mardana, Bakasura-vimardana, Putana-prana-hari; V7 — Rasa-leela, Yamuna-tira-vasi, Bansuri-dhara; V8 — Partha-sarathi, Gita-jnana-data, Vishvarupa-pradarshaka. The structure thus covers every dimension of Krishna — from infant to cowherd to king to philosopher. (Source: Krishna Mangalashtakam verses 1-8.)

seoFaqs.question: When and on what occasions should the Krishna Mangalashtakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishna Mangalashtakam is most powerful when recited on Wednesday (Budhvar — the day of Krishna and Vishnu), and is especially auspicious on Janmashtami (Bhadrapada Krishna Ashtami — Krishna's birth), Nanda Utsav (the day after Janmashtami), Radha Ashtami (Bhadrapada Shukla Ashtami), Govardhan Puja (Kartik Shukla Pratipada), Holi (Phalguna Purnima — celebrating Krishna's Vrindavan Holi), Vivah Panchami, Ekadashis, and during the entire month of Kartika (Damodar masa). It is also chanted at the start of any new venture, before journeys, at the inauguration of a new home, and as nityapaath by Krishna devotees of every Vaishnava sampradaya. (Source: Bhagavata mangala-paath tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting the Krishna Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti of the Krishna Mangalashtakam declares that the chanter becomes sarva-papa-vinirmukta (freed from all sins) and attains Goloka — Krishna's supreme abode. Traditional benefits also include: invocation of auspiciousness for new beginnings, marriages and family undertakings; invocation of all eight aspects of Krishna's leela for complete spiritual nourishment; awakening of bhakti and prema for Krishna and Radha; protection like the protection Krishna gave to the Vrindavan gopas; cultivation of Gita-jnana through the closing verse on Partha-sarathi; relief from Kali-yuga difficulties through Krishna's Govardhana-dharana grace; and the ultimate phala of Goloka-prapti. (Source: Krishna Mangalashtakam phalashruti.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Krishna Mangalashtakam differ from the Krishna Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishna Ashtakam (e.g., Adi Shankara's 'Bhaje Vrajaika Mandanam' or 'Vasudeva-sutam Devam') is primarily a darshana / dhyana stotra that paints meditative imagery of Krishna's form for contemplation. The Krishna Mangalashtakam, by contrast, is a mangala-vachana stotra — its specific function is to invoke auspiciousness, with the refrain Krishnaya cha mangalam at the close of every verse. Mangalashtakams are recited at ritual beginnings (samkalpa, vivaha, griha-pravesha, vyapara-arambha) where invoking shubha and mangala is the explicit purpose. Both genres are central to Krishna bhakti, but serve distinct ritual roles. (Source: classical mangala-stotra paddhati; Krishna stotra corpus.)
